perturbation.original_text: To the extent allowed by the law applicable to the Receiving Party, including, but not limited to, for UW -Oshkosh, Wis. Stat. §§ 893.82 & 895.46,” the Receiving Party will be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: To the extent allowed by the law applicable to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party will be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information. UW -Oshkosh may, at its sole discretion, choose to enforce Wis. Stat. §§ 893.82 & 895.46 in cases of a breach.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the specific Wisconsin Statutes regarding liability to a discretionary enforcement clause, the contract weakens the explicit legal obligation and makes it less clear that the university intends to consistently apply these statutes in cases of breach, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wisconsin Statute § 895.46 regarding state officer and employee immunity
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 895.46 mandates the state (and by extension, UW Oshkosh) to pay judgments against state employees acting within the scope of their employment. By making enforcement of related liability clauses discretionary, the modified text contradicts the spirit and potential application of this law, creating uncertainty about whether the state will uphold its responsibility to cover judgments.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language specifies that the Receiving Party "will be liable" to the extent allowed by law, citing specific Wisconsin statutes. The changed text makes the enforcement of those same statutes discretionary for UW-Oshkosh. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the explicit legal obligation initially present, directly contradicting the original intent to hold the Receiving Party liable under those statutes. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this weakening of the legal obligation, and the Law Explanation suggests this creates uncertainty regarding the state's responsibility. While no specific law snippets are provided to further solidify the contradiction, the shift from mandatory liability to discretionary enforcement, as articulated in the explanations, sufficiently establishes that the modified text contradicts the intent of the original contractual language by introducing a degree of optionality where there was previously a seemingly firm obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Wisconsin, U.S.A. without regard to conflicts of law provisions. In the event any legal action becomes necessary to enforce, the Parties agree that such action will be brought in the U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin, and the Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of said courts
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to comply with all applicable laws and regulations including U.S. export control. This Agreement shall be governed by the principles of fairness and mutual cooperation, with any disputes resolved through amicable negotiation. In the event any legal action becomes necessary to enforce, the Parties agree that such action may be brought in a venue determined by the defendant, and the Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of said courts. It is understood that Wisconsin law may be used as a reference.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from explicitly Wisconsin law to 'principles of fairness' significantly weakens the enforceability of the contract under specific, established legal precedents. Moreover, allowing the defendant to choose the venue introduces an element of forum shopping that may contradict established jurisdictional rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1391 outlines the general rules for venue in federal court. The modification granting the defendant the right to choose the venue may conflict with these established rules, particularly those concerning the defendant's residence, where the cause of action arose, or where property is located, potentially undermining the predictability and fairness of legal proceedings.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Wisconsin law and a specific venue (U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin). The modified text replaces Wisconsin law with 'principles of fairness' and allows the defendant to choose the venue, referencing Wisconsin law merely as a 'reference.' This contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which outlines specific rules for venue in federal courts. The modification granting the defendant venue choice undermines the established rules, which normally consider the defendant's residence or where the cause of action arose. As Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides... (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred...'. Granting the defendant unilateral venue selection contradicts these guidelines, creating a significant legal flaw.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to comply with all applicable laws and regulations including U.S. export control. The Disclosing Party agrees to notify the Receiving Party in writing prior to providing Receiving Party with access to any export regulated information and materials. Such notification shall include all associated classification numbers. The Receiving Party reserves the right to refuse receipt of any information or materials that are subject to export controls.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that all confidential information exchanged will be used in accordance with the Project and/or Purpose outlined herein. The parties reserve the right to engage in any activity whatsoever, without any duty to account to the Disclosing Party, which may relate to U.S. export control or similar regulatory obligations.
perturbation.explanation: By adding an arbitrary clause that permits both parties to ignore export control regulations in the context of the confidential information being used, the contract becomes vulnerable to challenge. This is because such controls are mandatory and not subject to agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically 15 CFR §§ 730-774, mandate compliance with export controls for items and information subject to U.S. jurisdiction. The modified clause, allowing parties to disregard export control regulations, directly contradicts these mandatory provisions, potentially exposing the parties to penalties and legal repercussions for non-compliance.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that allows parties to disregard U.S. export control regulations, directly contradicting the mandatory compliance requirements outlined in the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The Perturbation Explanation notes that export controls are mandatory and not subject to agreement, and the Law Explanation specifies that the EAR mandates compliance under 15 CFR §§ 730-774. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Export Administration Regulations under 15 CFR Chapter VII Subchapter C, which includes Parts 730-780, reinforcing the regulatory nature of export controls. Scraped Law Snippet 2 further clarifies that the EAR falls under 15 CFR 730-774. The change fundamentally undermines the legally required adherence to export control laws, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the prior permission of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise required by law. In the event that the Receiving Party is required by law to produce Confidential Information, the Receiving Party may disclose such Confidential Information without liability hereunder; provided, however, before producing any Confidential Information the Receiving Party shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the enforcement of any such requirement in order to provide the Disclosing Party with a reasonable amount of time so that the Disclosing Party may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy. In the event UW Oshkosh Revised 12/16/19 that such a protective order or other remedy is not obtained, or the Disclosing Party waives their right to obtain such an order or remedy, the Receiving Party may furnish only such portions of C onfidential Information as, pursuant to the advice of counsel, are required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the prior permission of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise required by law. In the event that the Receiving Party is required by law to produce Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will produce such information and shall have no liability towards the Disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the notification clause, the contract potentially violates due process principles. The Disclosing Party is deprived of the opportunity to seek a protective order, which is a standard legal remedy to safeguard confidential information when disclosure is legally compelled.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c) provides for protective orders to shield parties from undue burden or expense, including the disclosure of confidential information. By removing the notification requirement, the revised text effectively prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking such a protective order, potentially undermining their ability to protect sensitive information in legal proceedings and contravening the spirit of Rule 26(c).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party when legally compelled to disclose Confidential Information, which directly impacts the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order. The Law Explanation highlights Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c) which provides for protective orders. By removing the notification requirement, the revised text prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking such a protective order, potentially undermining their ability to protect sensitive information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure are meant "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Removing the notification clause hinders the 'just' determination, as it deprives the Disclosing party of a chance to protect their information.
